student_message: Is it a plus line? I don't remember. This is too hard.
final_message:
Take a deep breath, Riya. Math can definitely be hard sometimes, and it is completely okay to feel frustrated! 💛 Making mistakes or forgetting things is just a normal part of learning. I am still so proud of how beautifully you explained adding those numbers earlier. You are doing a wonderful job. 

Instead of a plus line, we call it a line of symmetry. Think about what happens when you physically fold a piece of paper along a line. Both sides should land exactly on top of each other! Did you know a butterfly has perfect reflection symmetry? 🦋 If you drew a line right down its middle and folded it, both wings would match up exactly. If you had to draw a line of symmetry on your own face to make both sides match, where do you think it would go?

orchestrator_intent.goal: Re-engage Riya, validate her feelings, and provide a gentle hint about lines of symmetry.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Riya is highly frustrated and distracted (looking away 32 times), repeatedly stating the work is too hard. She needs encouragement to ease her frustration, engagement to bring her focus back, and a simple, scaffolded hint to help her remember the concept without feeling overwhelmed.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: The session covered symmetry, symmetrical letters, and basic addition and subtraction. Riya visually understands symmetry, uses finger-counting, and correctly articulates her chosen operations. However, she struggles with math vocabulary, identifying symmetrical letters, and solving basic word problems. Her current learning state remains highly frustrated and overwhelmed. She frequently guesses answers, repeatedly states the work is "too hard," and lacks confidence, requiring constant emotional support and encouragement to persist.
